title: How to Build an Ethical Fashion Empire Without Burning Out
guestBio: Kathryn Letson is the founder of By The Sea Company, an ethical fashion brand that has grown to serve over 3,000 retailers. After experiencing burnout in NYC's high-pressure fashion industry, she pivoted to create a sustainable business model rooted in ergonomic design and eco-conscious textiles from her base in Kauai.
excerpt: Kathryn Letson turned her corporate fashion burnout into a breakthrough, building By The Sea Company into an ethical fashion empire with over 3,000 retailers. Discover how she created a business that aligns with her values while prioritizing wellbeing over hustle culture.
metaDescription: Learn how Kathryn Letson built an ethical fashion empire with 3,000+ retailers after NYC burnout. Tips for sustainable entrepreneurship and conscious business.
keyTakeaways: Burnout can become the catalyst for creating a business model that truly fits your life and values.
Ethical fashion means considering the entire supply chain, from materials to manufacturing to worker conditions.
You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something significant in business.
Starting small at farmers markets can be a powerful way to test and refine your business concept.
Living in alignment with your environment can significantly impact your creativity and leadership style.
Building a sustainable business requires designing systems that support both profit and principles.
The future of fashion lies in innovative, eco-conscious textiles that don't compromise on style or function.

pullQuotes.text: You don't have to sacrifice your wellbeing to build something big.

timestamps.label: First Steps in Launching By The Sea Company
Life in Kauai and Creative Leadership

content:
What if the breakdown you're avoiding is actually the breakthrough you need? In this powerful conversation with Kathryn Letson, founder of By The Sea Company, we explore how corporate burnout became the foundation for building an ethical fashion empire that serves over 3,000 retailers worldwide.

Kathryn's story begins in the high-pressure fashion houses of New York City, where the relentless pace and unsustainable practices left her questioning everything she thought she knew about success. Like so many of us, she found herself caught in the trap of believing that bigger, faster, and harder were the only paths to building something meaningful. But her body and spirit had other plans.

When Burnout Becomes Your Business Plan

The turning point came when Kathryn realized she could create her own template for success. Instead of continuing to pour herself into systems that drained her, she chose to design a business model that actually fit her life and values. This wasn't about scaling back her ambitions—it was about scaling them in a direction that made sense.

The move from Manhattan to Kauai wasn't just a change of scenery; it was a complete reimagining of what entrepreneurship could look like. Surrounded by the natural beauty of Hawaii, Kathryn began to understand how environment shapes creativity and leadership. The slower pace didn't mean less productivity—it meant more intentional productivity.

Starting at local farmers markets, she tested her concept of ergonomic design meets eco-conscious fashion. These weren't just sales opportunities; they were research labs where she could connect directly with customers and refine her vision. The feedback was immediate and honest, helping her build a foundation based on real needs rather than industry assumptions.

Defining Ethical Fashion in Action

One of the most eye-opening parts of this conversation centers on what ethical fashion actually means beyond the buzzwords. Kathryn breaks down the complexity of creating truly sustainable clothing, from sourcing eco-conscious textiles to ensuring fair labor practices throughout the supply chain.

This isn't about perfection—it's about progress and transparency. By The Sea Company's approach demonstrates that you can build a profitable business while prioritizing both people and planet. The key is being willing to do the hard work of researching suppliers, investing in quality materials, and educating customers about why these choices matter.

The ergonomic design element adds another layer to the sustainability conversation. When clothing is designed for comfort and movement, people wear it longer and more frequently. This challenges the fast fashion model not through shame or restriction, but by creating pieces so comfortable and versatile that customers naturally gravitate toward quality over quantity.

Building Systems That Support Your Values

What strikes me most about Kathryn's approach is how she's managed to scale her business without scaling her stress. Growing to over 3,000 retailers requires sophisticated systems and processes, but she's maintained the core principles that guided her from those early farmers market days.

This is where so many entrepreneurs get stuck—they assume that growth requires abandoning the values and practices that made their business special in the first place. Kathryn proves that the opposite is true. By staying committed to her principles around sustainability, worker welfare, and design excellence, she's created a brand that retailers and customers trust.

The geographic element plays a crucial role here too. Operating from Kauai forces a different relationship with time and urgency. While her business serves customers globally, her daily rhythm is grounded in island time, which creates space for reflection and strategic thinking that's often lost in the corporate hustle.

The Future of Conscious Commerce

Perhaps the most exciting part of this conversation is Kathryn's vision for the future of textiles and fabric innovation. She's not just participating in the sustainable fashion movement—she's helping to shape it. From exploring new eco-friendly materials to pioneering manufacturing processes that reduce environmental impact, By The Sea Company is proving that innovation and ethics can work hand in hand.

This forward-thinking approach extends to how she views business growth itself. Instead of the traditional model of expanding at all costs, she's focused on deepening relationships with existing retail partners and continuously improving product quality. This creates a more stable, sustainable business model that benefits everyone in the supply chain.

The lesson here isn't that everyone needs to move to a tropical island to build an ethical business. It's that we all have permission to question the conventional wisdom about how businesses should operate and what success should look like. Kathryn's journey from NYC burnout to ethical fashion leader demonstrates that when we align our work with our values, we create space for both personal fulfillment and professional impact.

As you listen to this conversation, I invite you to consider where your own burnout signals might be pointing toward breakthrough opportunities. What would it look like to design a business or career that supports your wellbeing while creating meaningful change in the world? Kathryn's story reminds us that we don't have to choose between success and sustainability—in fact, the most powerful businesses are often built when we refuse to make that compromise.

quiz.title: What's Your Sustainable Success Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach building something meaningful without burning out. Your results will reveal your unique path to creating impact while staying true to your values.

questions.question: When you're feeling overwhelmed at work, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Question whether the work itself aligns with what you really care about
Look for ways to systematize or streamline your current processes
Consider what needs to change or shift to make things more manageable
Step back and assess how this is affecting your overall life balance

questions.question: When starting a new project, you typically:

options.text: Research the deeper impact and ethical implications first
Create a detailed timeline and plan before moving forward
Start with what you have and adapt as you learn
Consider how it fits with your current life and energy levels

questions.question: Your definition of business success includes:

options.text: Making a positive difference in the world through your work
Building something stable and sustainable for the long term
Having the flexibility to evolve and respond to opportunities
Maintaining your health and relationships while growing professionally

questions.question: When you hear about someone's rapid business growth, you think:

options.text: I wonder what corners they might have cut to get there so fast
That's great for them, but I prefer building methodically
I'm curious about what strategies I could adapt for my situation
I hope they're not sacrificing their wellbeing for those results
Reflect your values and allow you to do meaningful work
Have clear structures and systems that support efficient progress
Offer variety and room to pivot based on what's working
Support your whole self—creativity, relationships, and personal growth

questions.question: When facing a major life transition, you're most likely to:

options.text: Use it as an opportunity to realign with your core values
Plan carefully and take measured steps toward your new direction
Embrace the change and see where it leads you
Consider how to make the transition in a way that honors your whole life

results.title: The Conscious Pioneer
results.description: You're driven by purpose and aren't afraid to challenge the status quo when something doesn't align with your values. You naturally think about the bigger picture and long-term impact of your decisions. Sometimes you worry that prioritizing ethics means sacrificing growth, but you're learning that authentic success takes time. This episode will show you exactly how to build something significant while staying true to your principles—Kathryn's journey proves you don't have to choose between impact and integrity.

results.title: The Intentional Builder
results.description: You believe in steady, thoughtful growth over flashy overnight success. You're naturally drawn to creating systems and processes that can sustain long-term success. You sometimes question if you're moving fast enough compared to others, but your methodical approach is actually your superpower. Kathryn's story of starting at farmers markets and building to 3,000+ retailers will inspire you to trust your measured approach and see how intentional growth creates lasting impact.

results.title: The Adaptive Innovator
results.description: You're incredibly resourceful and know how to pivot when life demands it. You've probably had to reinvent yourself before and you're not afraid of change when it serves your growth. You sometimes wonder if you're being too reactive instead of proactive, but your flexibility is a rare gift in business. This episode will resonate deeply as Kathryn shares how her biggest breakdown became her breakthrough—showing you how to turn life's curveballs into your competitive advantage.

results.title: The Holistic Harmonizer
results.description: You instinctively know that true success includes your wellbeing, relationships, and personal fulfillment—not just business metrics. You're always seeking balance and integration rather than compartmentalization. You might worry that your need for harmony makes you less ambitious, but you're actually ahead of the curve. Kathryn's transition from Manhattan burnout to building her empire from Kauai will speak directly to your soul and validate that your whole-life approach to success is exactly what the world needs.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women into powerful tangible leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur, and mama of six. 

Today's guest is someone I met in the most manager way possible, not in a boardroom or on a press tour, but at a farmers market on the lush northshore of Kauai. I was drawn to her work instantly, her presence, her purpose, her designs, and when I heard her story, I knew we had to bring her here.

Kathryn Letson is the founder of By the Sea Company, an ethical fashion brand rooted in sustainability, innovation, and wellness. But before she was designing earth-conscious clothes worn by thousands, she was in the heart of the New York fashion world, working for large corporations, designing under pressure, and ultimately burning out. Instead of walking away, she walked forward. She left the chaos behind and built something different. A fashion business on her own terms. One that now supplies over 3,000 retailers, promotes ergonomic design and experiments with futuristic textiles. She's joining us remotely from her home in Kauai where her lifestyle and her business now flow in harmony.

So, if you've ever wanted to hear what happens when a woman combines creativity, conscience, and courage, you're in for a treat. All right, Kathryn, I'm so glad that you're here today. Thanks so much for being on the show. I really appreciate you.

**Kathryn:** Thank you so much for having me. I'm honored to meet you.

**Aimee:** Thank you and I love how our worlds collided at the Wipa Farmers Market and I'm so glad you're here. So, let's start at the beginning. What pulled you into fashion originally?

**Kathryn:** Well, and I love this message for all people, all women. I'm an artist at my core. I'm an artist. So part of me is like it doesn't matter what I do, I have to do art. So I just happen to choose fashion because I love fashion. I just love fashion. I always have.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And you think about what you did when you were little. I played dress up a lot.

**Kathryn:** Yeah. Totally. I remember that too. I totally do of playing. We had this conversation on the phone and so you're dressing up your dolls and I was making radio shows with Kelly.

**Aimee:** That's and that's what I tell everybody when they're like, "Well, I don't know what I'm doing or what to do." Like, "Well, what do you love to do? What did you do when you were little and no one's watching? No one's paying you. That's your true joy."

**Kathryn:** So, for me, bottom line is it's creating things. But I just have such an enormous passion for textiles, really good textiles, and I want to share that.

**Aimee:** Yeah. You have a distinct vantage point on textiles. We'll get into it for sure. So, you worked for major fashion corporations in New York. Can you share a little bit about that chapter of your life? What did you learn and what ultimately made you leave?

**Kathryn:** Yeah, sure. I'm a classically trained fashion designer who graduated from Parsons in New York City and was scouted immediately by Ralph Lauren and Donna Karan and people like that and so ended up working with Ralph Lauren for a little bit and then went off to Abercrombie and Fitch later, Gap later. I started Abercrombie Girls with another woman which was an exciting project too.

So, I had some very big projects back in the day which were amazing because they taught me from start to finish how to create something.

**Aimee:** Oh, what a cool opportunity to have something like that. We always say if you want to learn to do something, go work for a large corporation. They'll teach you how to do it and then go out on your own and do it for yourself.

**Kathryn:** Well, they're professionals and like for instance, I have high respect for the Gap. They are a solid company that's been around for a very long time and I learned a ton working at that job and working with Mickey Drexler.

**Aimee:** It's kind of amazing when you mentioned that you were in high fashion in New York and now I meet you on the north shore of Kauai and you're in your stand and you've got all your beautiful clothes and I'm thinking okay there I got to hear this story like how do you go from here to there and I get it because all the women I talk to who have worked in corporate, the burnout is real and it usually happens right when you should be at the height of your career.

**Kathryn:** Oh, seriously. I mean, it and for me it did. It did. I had full burnout. I was really sick with autoimmune stuff. Happy autoimmune Sjogren's disease and all these things. I'm like, what is happening? What is going on with me? Well, when you live out of alignment with the truth of your authenticity of who you are, that's going to happen. And that's what I was doing. So yeah, course correction was necessary and required.

**Aimee:** Yeah. And the corporations that you're working for were built by men and very much built by men run by men type of corporate structure that you're in. I feel it's very hard for women to work in those kinds of environments. I feel burnout is very common. It's one of the things that really is one of the big questions that I have is, okay, so why are women burning out at the peak of their careers at these large corporations? We see women that just get to where they should be at the tippity top and they just can't take it anymore. They're sick. They're tired. They've lost the passion for what got them going. They've lost their purpose. They're way down a path that no longer suits them.

**Kathryn:** I can tell you why. Because you give it your all. It's a corporate job. You've been vetted maybe for a year, two years that it's not a joke how they interview in the fashion industry. They may interview you for a year. So, you give it your all and that never seems to be enough. And it's a hierarchy. So, if you give it like a pyramid, right? It's a hierarchy and climbing that corporate ladder is just it's insane to me. Now, the idea of the structure of the hierarchy just doesn't that creates the burnout.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Kathryn:** To me, because I don't think that corporations can't keep working harder and harder and harder and harder. You can't.

**Aimee:** Exactly. It's the patriarchal yeah. It should get easier. Yes, it should. And it does. When you do it for yourself, it does get easier. You build something, it takes inertia to build something and then you kind of have built it and it's a garden and now it grows itself. You've planted all the seeds, you've tilled the soil, you've done the work. But I think it's the difference between the way that women do business and the way the men do business and maybe even the matriarchy and the patriarchy is this idea of the climbing, climbing the ladder versus the fact that life is really this garden that we grow.

Are we investing the idea that every job in the company is important and that one job isn't more important than the other. I have an ethical problem with that. And that is another reason I left corporate. And when I hire people and work with people, it's you work with me. You don't work for me. I don't ever want to create a hierarchy.

**Aimee:** Agreed. Yeah. I always feel very maternal to all of my employees. I always feel like I want them to be happy.

**Kathryn:** Yes. Yes, I do. Yeah. In a very motherly way. I want to make sure everybody's very happy and content and feels like they're very much in their purpose and that they're also in their lane and operating in their realm of genius, because I think that's so important that nobody feels like they're trying to do something they're not good at.

**Aimee:** Now you're empowering them and now it's a cooperative situation. And to me, that's win-win all the way around. And I like to work like that, too.

**Kathryn:** Yeah.

**Aimee:** I feel like most of the women I talk to have this sort of structure within their companies where the CEO, the founder sort of as more of this matriarchal figure within the company that's sort of just overseeing that everyone has been cared for, nurtured, and is growing in their positions. It's like the way that you're describing is like you're the mother of the business and you are to nurture what you're growing there. And doesn't that make perfect sense?

**Kathryn:** Yeah. Like it's a whole different way of doing business than how men do business. And this is a woman-owned and run and I hire women-owned and run businesses as well. This is a woman power business right here.

**Aimee:** Same here. I do my best to keep my vertical of partners, as best I can. We really try to keep it in the family with all the women in business. I'm very much surrounded by women in business and having this show, I realized my gosh, do I know a lot of women in business for what you're doing.

**Kathryn:** Everything. I think it's awesome. And when I come I do come to LA sometimes for wholesale trade shows downtown LA and it's mostly women and I just love the energy.

**Aimee:** Oh yeah. Totally. I know. It's just So tell me what gave you the clarity and the courage to start by the sea company?

**Kathryn:** I think the burnout which led to the path that I had been shown and given which was you go to college and then you get a good job XYZ this is what you do kind of thing. I was like that didn't work for me. Okay, that didn't work. So I'm probably one of the most creative people I've ever met. And so I'm going to think very differently and I'm going to think, okay, well, I should have had my own business a long time ago, but I'll forgive myself for staying in the fashion industry for so long.

I have these conversations with my corporate friends. And it was really about when I left the biggest question when I left corporate is what do you want to spend your time doing? And when I counsel other women and other people, this is the two questions I ask. What do you want to spend the majority of your time doing? And what was your passion when you were little?

Put those two things together. I was trading my raggedy toys when I was seven. I was in the rag trades since I was little. So if you really pay attention to who you were when you were little, you can figure out your the path of your career and the highest joy that you can achieve in your work.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Totally. Yeah. Reconnecting to our original purpose which we I think as children we knew and we forget as we get older because it gets whipped out of you by society, by corporate, by family and the education system too. Sometimes, oh no, you shouldn't do that.

**Kathryn:** Yeah, limited thinking.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. So, tell me, what was the very first product that you made or sold with your own company? And do you remember the moment you knew this was going to work?

**Kathryn:** Definitely. And I started my business in 2007 as by the Sea Organics and then started that's wholesale and started the retail branch at by the sea company.com both at the same time. But they both started with kantha quilts and totes. And if you don't know what kantha is, it's out of India. I've got a placemat sitting in front of me. It's an ancient art which is empowering women's stitching circles. So this is all women who are stitching.

**Aimee:** Wow. Beautiful. So pretty. The first products were kantha quilts and kantha totes? Wow, that's so cool. And how did you know that this was going to work?

**Kathryn:** I think not until I started selling things did I know that. I mean intuitively as I worked also a long time after I left designing and started working as a fashion trend researcher and then giving large presentations in Las Vegas about fabrics and trend forecasting. I know what's coming and this is what's been coming for a very long time. So now we're positioned nice.

It's about really diving into the passion of organic living and when you understand that eating organic food is essential real organic food there's also fake organic food out there right oh yeah the real organic food then you start understanding about the clothing that you wear that your largest organ is your skin right so what I'm wearing linen today. So, what you put on your skin really, really matters. Really matters to your health.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. I have your organic cotton shirt on today, actually. It's so comfy. It's so soft.

**Kathryn:** It's about cozy with me and with us. It's about cozy.

**Aimee:** It really is. I mean, I could wear this shirt every day if society would allow it. It's that comfy and cozy. It's really fabulous. But most people don't realize that most of the fabrics that we're wearing right now are synthetic. They're made which means they're made from plastic products, which means somebody put some of that gasoline in their tank of their car and someone put it on as underpants.

**Kathryn:** That's right. And that's raw. That's real talk right there. That you're giving real talk because working at the farmers market. I'm going to say even at the farmers market, at the airport, I'm going to say I see 70% women wearing yoga pants. Farmers market, 30% women yoga shorts or pants. Same thing. You're forcing microfibers into your largest organ. That's not a good idea at all.

**Aimee:** Yeah. All the microplastics or now they're showing them they're in our veins. They're everywhere within our bodies and we are absorbing them. So a little more mentally ill.

**Kathryn:** Yes. Now you got something going on.

**Aimee:** Interrupt things.

**Kathryn:** Yeah. Endocrine disruptors, all kinds of and we're I mean from cancer to you name it. Endocrine disruptors can affect people's fertility, which we're now seeing, I think I just read a study that a 17-year-old man of today has the testosterone level of a 70-year-old man from the 1970s.

**Aimee:** Oh my goodness. Wow. Wow.

**Kathryn:** Isn't that crazy? But it's just we are living in the petro times, whether we have petroleum on our food, we have petroleum in our food, we have petroleum in our clothes, in our household products, our toothpaste. I mean, our kids are putting it on with deodorant. They're spraying it on them as fragrance. I mean, it's really very toxic.

Well, thank you for spreading awareness. I'm very much that. And when you came to Kauai, this place is completely organic. You got the organic Gestapo that lives here. Like it is not a joke over here and you're in California. I know you got some of the same thing going on. But the point is we really want to be healthy and we really want to shift and change our lives. And that's why I left New York City. Toxic behavior, toxic everything and just all of that to live and create an organic life and then share that passion with others.

**Aimee:** But yeah, you chose a beautiful spot to do it. Northshore of Kauai is absolutely special and magical.

**Kathryn:** Really is. Not a day goes by that I don't give thanks.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. You're in a very beautiful place. So you've said before, we were talking about how you wanted to create an ethical fashion template and what does that mean to you and how do you bring that to life in your brand?

**Kathryn:** Yeah. So I've got 30 years in the fashion industry. So for the past couple years, I've been starting to put together what I'm calling a template on how to produce and do ethical fashion, but you're purchasing internationally. You could produce in America, but you could choose where you produce. It's to me it's about how you do it. Are you doing it ethically? And if you do produce in another country, then you have to go there. There's no negotiation about that. You have to go see who's doing it, how they're doing it, how they're treating people. That is how it happens. It's not this big mystery about that.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Kathryn:** So for me, I've been creating a one, two, three. I have a very systematic organized brain set kind of teacher mind brain set of that. So I'm figuring out how to put that together and at the same time I've just hired a new assistant two weeks ago who said Kathryn I know you want to do your own podcast and I love that. As I'm gearing up for my own podcast to teach this to teach other women or people whoever but women who to empower women because I also want to add in here that I feel I've thought deeply about this and I feel that one of the big shifts in our society one of the shifts that we can make is to work for yourself stop working for a corporation work for yourself only if that feels right that's not right for everybody some people really need structure. They really need someone else structuring their life. I did not need that. Someone else telling me when to be there and what to do. That used to make me nuts.

So, if you're like that, let that be your let this be your sign that oh god, maybe I should have my own business kind of thing. I think that it's the one big game changer and I think it's one of the things that as we move into the fourth industrial revolution that technology is now really very available for people to have their own companies and as things are automated there will be less mundane task work within the corporate world. So a lot of people will be freed up to get creative.

**Aimee:** Yeah, there should be a shift, workforce shift. And I know personally know three people in my life making this shift from a corporate job to their own work.

**Kathryn:** That's amazing. That's amazing. I know. I just had the girls from Unicorn at Every Stall and they did the same thing. They one of them had been at Pepsi for 27 years and the other one had been working in the fashion industry in New York. They're cousins. And they teamed up and started this great company for themselves. They were burnt out. Burnt out. Couldn't do it. Same age, right when you should be at the peak of your career and you just can't take one more step forward in that corporation.

And again, I feel like that's a sign from the universe that you're not living in alignment because when I can vouch for it, when you do get in alignment, I'm not burning out. I do reach a point where I'm like, "Oh, time to take a break." But then I do because I work for myself. And there's not the burnout again that's caused by that pushing pushing pushing because you're in the hierarchy and you're trying to get to the next level.

**Aimee:** Yeah, totally. There was a moment of clarity.

**Kathryn:** Yes, absolutely. Yeah. It's like oh you just feel like you're missing there was something about your purpose that just didn't quite align with this specific mission and I'm so glad you made the jump because I think every woman who makes the leap it's like one of us then we can inspire another woman and she can take that leap and we can help free women. Because I really just don't think that the corporate lifestyle is conducive for the female lifestyle in general. I feel like women are having a very hard time with it.

**Aimee:** I agree. I struggled greatly and men were always at the top. They were I noticed.

**Kathryn:** Yeah. And I think that we're looking at a structure that was built in a time when these men who built this had wives at home who cared for their home and their children. We're looking at something that was built from a long time ago, something that's archaic. Now we're expecting women to go into the office and still go home and do the laundry and do the grocery shopping and cook the dinner and pick the kids up and do all No wonder we're burnt out. We are trying to do too much.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Exactly. Yeah. You can't work 40 hours a week in a building and then care for your family in a way as if you were a stay-at-home mom. That's just not possible. But I think also, women have just felt like that for a couple generations now, a good 20 10 couple 20 years. Maybe even 30. Who could do this? Who could do all of this? And I knew people in New York doing that. I'm like, are you And they had they all had nannies.

**Kathryn:** Okay. Yes. They all had you have to. Yes. But still you could afford a nanny all the time. I do not have children because I was deep into my career for so long.

**Aimee:** Oh, yeah. Totally. And I thought it was what was demanded of you. You know, it was either you're going to have a nanny and someone else is going to raise your kids or you don't have children. Those are your options. They didn't see their kids and then they come home late because you have to work late. You're at 10:00. Your kids are sleeping. You have to leave early. You're gone. I was like, "No."

**Kathryn:** It's really really sad. And then I'm able to talk to women who run their own companies and it's like, okay, so I schedule my stuff. If my kid has this thing I want to go to, I just don't schedule my clients around that time. All of a sudden you take your life back. Your time because what do we really is it the money or is it the time? It's really the time.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Working for yourself. You own your time. When you work for someone else, you sold your time to them. You work for you. You decide we decide to meet up right now. It's our time.

**Kathryn:** Absolutely. And I feel like I'm a very lucky girl. I grew up had two very entrepreneurial parents and that was very much the model that was shown to me as a child, although I felt like my mother very much tried to work like a man and she would work herself so hard and try to emulate the hours that a man would put in and she would always end up very very sick.

**Aimee:** That was the model, she didn't have a female role model saying, "No, take a break."

**Kathryn:** Yeah. She didn't have a period today. Go rest.

**Aimee:** Right. Exactly. Oh, or your kids have a school play. Go. You should go to that. You're going to regret not going to that. That right there is balance. And when you work for yourself, no one's telling you not to balance. "Oh, I have to go take care of that now." You do.

**Kathryn:** Yeah. Yeah, you do. And we're getting so much better at it, too. As women, I feel like we're finally I had a man I was doing a documentary on a woman in film and Amy Holden Jones. I was in film school and I was interviewing Haskell Wexler. He's a very famous cinematographer from the 70s and he said, "Amy, women need to learn how to do business as women. They need to stop imitating men and they need to do things the way that women do things." And I was 22 years old when he said that to me and it struck me because I saw my mother very much emulating the way that men were doing things and I saw how it wasn't working for her. I really saw how it was making her sick and burning her out.

And I think that's one of the things that in my life I always really tried to have a little more honor for my femaleness, for my the cycles of my life, for when I'm pregnant.
